返回本批次

美国西部三月的高温导致积雪处于历史最低水平

March heat in American west has left snowpack at record-low levels

www.theguardian.com 作者 ijidak 分数 102 评论 56 2026/04/02 23:47
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 本周在美国西部进行的降雪调查给出了严峻的预测，此前经历了历史性的暖冬和三月的酷热气温，导致该地区的临界积雪处于创纪录的低水平。专家警告说，尽管热量开始消退，但过去一个月令人震惊的融化速度使主要盆地在未来的旱季中处于未知的领域。尽管预测中仍有可能出现更多降雪，但专家表示降雪可能太少也太晚了。“今年完全是另一个水平，”科罗拉多州立大学气候学家拉斯·舒马赫博士在谈到三月份开始迅速融化本已稀疏的积雪的高温时说道。 “今年远远低于我们掌握的数据的任何其他年份，这非常令人担忧。” 作为某种节水账户，积雪对于供水至关重要。 4 月 1 日这一周在西部地区进行的测量被视为夏季水量峰值的重要指标，这些水量可能会融化到水库、河流和小溪以及整个夏季的干渴地区。2026 年 4 月 1 日星期三，加州水资源部在菲利普斯站进行降雪调查时，安迪·雷辛 (Andy Reising)、吉姆·香农 (Jim Shannon) 和雅各布·科伦 (Jacob Kollen) 发现降雪量为零。这是自 2015 年在内华达山脉菲利普斯站降雪调查以来的第二低值。照片：Paul Kitagaki Jr/ZUMA Press Wire/Shutterstock 周三，在加利福尼亚州内华达山脉进行一项重要调查时，州政府官员试图测量微薄的积雪，透过薄薄的白色拼凑物可以看到草和泥。“通常我们会站在这里，”加州水资源雪调查和供水预测部门经理安迪·雷辛（Andy Reising）指着下巴的高度说道。 4 月 1 日，这个 5 英尺高的工具通常会深入高高的护堤，戳进他旁边的棕色土地。 “实际上没有可测量的降雪。”赖辛说，由于深度为零，含水量为零，今年在菲利普斯站进行的年度四月降雪调查是有记录以来第二严重的，仅次于2015年，当时官员“走过一片干燥的田野”。专家们担心的不仅仅是山顶上的积雪量，还有山顶上仍然冻结的水分量。 “雪水当量”(SWE) 是衡量融化后供应自然和人造系统的指标，其值极低。加利福尼亚州内华达山脉的积雪。资料来源：美国宇航局。加利福尼亚州内华达山脉的积雪。资料来源：美国国家航空航天局。根据该州水资源部门的数据，周三，加利福尼亚州内华达山脉的 SWE 含量仅为 4.9 英寸，为平均值的 18%。在科罗拉多河源头，这个为多个州超过 4000 万人口、550 万英亩农业区、30 个部落国家和墨西哥部分地区提供水源的重要流域，周一的 SWE 含量略高于 4 英寸，为平均值的 24%。这还不到之前被认为是创纪录低点的一半。舒马赫表示，即将到来的风暴可能会减缓早期融化速度，但不足以将盆地从边缘拉回来。舒马赫表示，经过数月的融化后，进入四月份的雪水当量测量值达到了通常在五月或六月出现的水平。这个问题非常普遍。美国农业部 (USDA) 一个部门根据 1991 年至 2020 年的水平记录了平均值，数据显示西南部和西部山间各州的气温低得令人瞠目结舌。周一，大盆地的气温仅为平均水平的 16%，科罗拉多州下游地区（包括亚利桑那州的大部分地区和内华达州的部分地区）为 10%。覆盖新墨西哥州、得克萨斯州和科罗拉多州部分地区的里奥格兰德河的积雪率为 8%。舒马赫说：“今年的情况有可能比过去的任何一年都要糟糕。”科罗拉多州落基山脉的积雪。资料来源：美国宇航局。科罗拉多落基山脉的积雪。资料来源：美国宇航局。据联邦分析人士称，尽管西部大部分地区的降水量接近正常，但该地区的每个主要河流流域在三月份开始时都在努力应对雪旱。根据 3 月 8 日编制的最新联邦雪旱更新数据，大约 91% 的气象站报告的雪水当量低于中值。水资源管理者和气候专家一直对三月份的奇迹抱有希望——一场强冷风暴可能会让该地区走上正轨。相反，一场与一年中这个时候有记录不同的酷热热浪烘烤了该地区，并引发了快速融化。“三月通常是暴风雪的大月份，”舒马赫说。 “我们通常期望的不是下雪，而是前所未有的、超乎寻常的温暖。” 3 月份，超过 1,500 项月度高温记录被打破，还有数百项被打破。该事件“可能是美国有史以来观察到的统计上最异常的极端高温事件之一”气候科学家丹尼尔·斯温（Daniel Swain）在本周发布的一份分析报告中表示，“除了这一切明显的‘怪异’之外，我们破纪录的三月高温带来的最严重影响可能是几乎整个美国西部2025-26年积雪的大量减少。”斯温称高温造成的损失“简直令人震惊”，他指出，加州的情况与此持平。尽管最高海拔地区仍然覆盖着白色，但“较低的山坡现在几乎全州都完全裸露了”。斯温表示，山脉的积雪融化速度如此之快，如果按照目前的速度继续下去，到四月初就将所剩无几。它不太可能保持这种惊人的速度，但该州仍有很大可能出现有记录以来最早的融化。“感觉就像我们今年跳过了春天，直接进入了夏天。” “热浪，”DWR 主任卡拉·内梅斯 (Karla Nemeth) 在周三的新闻发布会上说道，“本来应该逐渐融化的积雪在几周前突然发生了。”她补充说，今年的调查是他们进行的最快的调查之一。但随着气温上升，已经出现了显着的变化。“今年的特征是加州未来预计会出现更多的许多因素：冬季雨量增多，雪量减少，以及持续炎热和干燥的天气，”赖辛在测量前向《卫报》提供的一份声明中表示。结果出来后，他指出，自 2007 年以来，4 月 1 日积雪量最低的事件有六次。2 月至 3 月间，大盐湖周围出现了积雪。资料来源：美国宇航局。二月至三月期间，大盐湖周围会出现降雪。资料来源：美国宇航局。然而，由于一系列强降雨，加利福尼亚州的水库蓄水量几乎都超出了历史平均水平。虽然这将有助于支持供水，但也意味着快速融化的积雪可能更难捕获。在科罗拉多河流域，情况可能更加严峻。科罗拉多河上最大的两个水库是米德湖和鲍威尔湖，它们合计占蓄水量的 90% 左右，截至 3 月 29 日，水库蓄水量分别为 25% 和 33%，几乎没有水可以填满。由于预计水位会迅速下降，官员们已经开始搬迁鲍威尔湖上的一个浮动码头，因为专家警告说，这座重要水库的水位可能会降至 20 世纪 60 年代蓄水以来的最低水位。如果它们跌得足够远，系统将完全停止运行。所谓的“死池”——当水位不足以通过水坝、产生水力发电并分配到下游时——将是灾难性的。科罗拉多河已经透支了一个多世纪，但气温上升和降水减少给西部城市、农场、工业和野生动物所依赖的系统带来了更大的压力。极端的情况使围绕谁将首当其冲地承担急需的削减的紧张谈判变得更加紧迫和紧张。已经过了两个关键最后期限的七个州在如何管理这条河流的重要资源以度过更炎热和干燥的未来方面仍陷入僵局。但可怕的积雪数量迫使一些城市提前启动用水限制。犹他州盐湖城当地官员呼吁居民和企业开始节约用水，目标是减少 1000 万加仑，同时城市设施将限制 10% 的使用量。科罗拉多州各地都有限制草坪浇水的地方命令，而怀俄明州的居民则被警告称，最早可能在五月全面限制户外灌溉。西部地区的农民和牧场主也必须做出艰难的决定和重大调整，减少水的分配，并认识到供应将会紧张。火灾季节的前景令人不安，快速融化的积雪预计将对饮用水供应、农业生产和户外休闲产生深远影响。它还可能为更大的火灾奠定基础。科罗拉多大学博尔德分校合作研究所的高级副科学家乔尔·利森比博士说：“除非天气模式发生重大变化，并且我们以某种方式实现了某种奇迹般的春季降水，否则我们正在考虑延长火灾季节。”他指出，积雪和火灾之间并不存在一对一的关系，但它们是相互关联的。“在任何类型的火灾情况下，你都需要一些火花或点火装置，”他说。通常在雪层保护下停留更长时间的景观将变得更容易燃烧。他说，火灾季节可能“比我们通常预期的提前几周到几个月开始”。 “高温和低积雪将导致周围植被迅速干燥，从而导致提前开始。”Reising、Nemeth、Jim Shannon 和 Jacob Kollen 发现2026 年 4 月 1 日菲利普斯站降雪测量结果为零。这是自 2015 年内华达山脉菲利普斯站降雪测量以来的第二低值。摄影：Paul Kitagaki Jr/ZUMA Press Wire/Shutterstock 最近几周，在极端高温和低湿度的刺激下，西部山间和高原平原已经爆发了数十起大规模破坏性火灾。今年美国各地已经有超过 150 万英亩的土地被烧毁，是 10 年平均水平的两倍多。虽然舒马赫表示，他预计今年将是最引人注目的一年，但气候危机正在加剧变暖趋势，气候科学家长期以来警告说，这将使西部变得更加炎热和干燥。美国西部的降雪季节正在缩短，而高火灾风险将持续数月。克拉克大学气候学家兼助理教授艾比·弗雷泽博士说：“气候变化将导致许多极端事件恶化。”她补充说，危险重叠或快速连续发生的复合事件正在增加。今年的高温和干旱形成了一对二的打击，将共同产生更大的火灾危险。她强调需要采取变革性行动，并优先考虑适应和缓解。 “看到这一切按照我们长期以来的预测进行，真是令人心碎，”她说。 “我们为自己准备的改变将是灾难性的。”

Original Summary

  1. Snow surveys taking place across the American west this week are offering a grim prognosis, after a historically warm winter and searing March temperatures left the critical snowpack at record-low levels across the region.Experts warned that even as the heat begins to subside, the stunning pace of melt-off over the past month has left key basins in uncharted territory for the dry seasons ahead. Though there’s still potential for more snow in the forecast, experts said it will probably be too little too late.“This year is on a whole other level,” said Dr Russ Schumacher, a Colorado State University climatologist, speaking about the intense heat that began rapidly melting the already sparse snowpack in March. “Seeing this year so far below any of the other years we have data for is very concerning.”Acting as a water savings account of sorts, snowpacks are essential to water supply. Measurements taken across the west during the week of 1 April are viewed as important indicators of the peak amounts of water that might melt into reservoirs, rivers and streams and across thirsty landscapes through the summer.Andy Reising, Jim Shannon and Jacob Kollen found a zero measurement of snow during the California department of water resources snow survey at Phillips Station on Wednesday, 1 April 2026. This is the second lowest since 2015 during snow survey at the Phillips Station in the Sierra Nevada. Photograph: Paul Kitagaki Jr/ZUMA Press Wire/ShutterstockDuring a critical survey in California’s Sierra Nevada on Wednesday, grass and mud could be seen through the thin white patchwork as state officials attempted to measure the meager snowpack.“Normally we’d be standing right here,” Andy Reising, manager of California department of water resource’s snow surveys and water supply forecasting unit said, gesturing at chin height. The 5ft-tall tool typically thrust deep into the high berms on 1 April poked into the brown earth next to him. “There is actually no measurable snow.”With zero depth and zero water content, this year’s annual April snow survey conducted at Phillips Station, was the second worst on record, beaten only by 2015 when officials “walked across a dry field”, Reising said.It’s not just the amount of snow left on mountaintops that’s concerning experts, but the amount of moisture still frozen within them. “Snow water equivalent” (SWE), a measurement of what could melt off to supply natural and manmade systems, is exceptionally low.Snowpack in California’s Sierra Nevada. Source: Nasa. Snowpack in California’s Sierra Nevada. Source: Nasa.California’s Sierra Nevada had just 4.9in of SWE, or 18% of average on Wednesday, according to the state’s department of water resources.In the Colorado River headwaters, an important basin that supplies more than 40 million people across several states, along with 5.5m acres of agriculture, 30 tribal nations, and parts of Mexico, had just over 4in of SWE on Monday, or 24% of average. That’s less than half what was previously considered the record low.Schumacher said the incoming storm could slow the early melting but won’t be enough to pull the basins back from the brink. Snow water equivalent measurements going into April were at levels typically seen in May or June, after months of melt-off, according to Schumacher.The issue is extremely widespread. Data from a branch of the US Department of Agriculture (USDA), which logs averages based on levels between 1991 and 2020, shows states across the south-west and intermountain west with eye-popping lows. The Great Basin had only 16% of average on Monday and the lower Colorado region, which includes most of Arizona and parts of Nevada, was at 10%. The Rio Grande, which covers parts of New Mexico, Texas and Colorado, was at 8%.“This year has the potential of being way worse than any of the years we have analogues for in the past,” Schumacher said.The snowpack in the Colorado Rocky Mountains. Source: Nasa.The snowpack in the Colorado Rocky Mountains. Source: Nasa.‘Nothing short of shocking’Even with near-normal precipitation across most of the west, every major river basin across the region was grappling with snow drought when March began, according to federal analysts. Roughly 91% of stations reported below-median snow water equivalent, according to the last federal snow drought update compiled on 8 March. Water managers and climate experts had been hopeful for a March miracle – a strong cold storm that could set the region on the right track. Instead, a blistering heatwave unlike any recorded for this time of year baked the region and spurred a rapid melt-off.“March is often a big month for snowstorms,” Schumacher said. “Instead of getting snow we would normally expect we got this unprecedented, way-off-the-scale warmth.”More than 1,500 monthly high temperature records were broken in March and hundreds more tied. The event was “likely among the most statistically anomalous extreme heat events ever observed in the American south-west”, climate scientist Daniel Swain said in an analysis posted this week.“Beyond the conspicuous ‘weirdness’ of it all,” Swain added, “the most consequential impact of our record-shattering March heat will likely be the decimation of the water year 2025-26 snowpack across nearly all of the American west.”Calling the toll left by the heat “nothing short of shocking”, Swain noted that California was tied for its worst mountain snowpack value on record. While the highest elevations are still coated in white, “lower slopes are now completely bare nearly statewide”.The snow is melting so fast in the Sierra that, if it continues at its current rate, little would be left by early April. It’s unlikely to keep up this astounding pace, but there’s still high potential for the earliest melt-off on record in the state, according to Swain.“It feels like we skipped spring this year and dropped straight into a summer heatwave,” said Karla Nemeth, the DWR director, during Wednesday’s briefing. “What should be gradual snowmelt happened suddenly weeks ago.” This year’s was one of the quickest surveys they’d had, she added.But with warmth on the rise, there has already been a notable shift.“This year has featured many of the factors California is expected to see more of in the future: winters with more rain and less snow and stretches of hot and dry conditions,” Reising said, in a statement provided to the Guardian before the measurement. After the results were in, he noted that six of the lowest 1 April snowpacks have occurred since 2007.Snow around the Great Salt Lake seen between February versus March. Source: Nasa. Snow around the Great Salt Lake seen between February versus March. Source: Nasa.California’s reservoirs are nearly all filled beyond their historic averages, however, thanks to a series of robust rains. While this will help support water supplies, it will also mean fast-melting snow may be harder to capture.In the Colorado River Basin, the situation could be even more dire. The two largest reservoirs on the Colorado River are Lake Mead and Lake Powell, which together account for about 90% of storage, are 25% and 33% full accordingly, as of 29 March, and there is little to fill them.Already officials are in the process of relocating a floating marina on Lake Powell in anticipation of the quickly receding water levels, as experts warn the vital reservoir could drop to the lowest levels recorded since it was filled in the 1960s. If they fall far enough, the system would cease to function altogether. So-called “deadpool” – when water isn’t high enough to pass through the dams, generate hydroelectric power, and be distributed downriver – would be catastrophic.The Colorado River has been overdrawn for more than a century but rising temperatures and lower precipitation are putting more pressure on the system that depended on by cities, farms, industries and wildlife across the west. The extreme conditions have added more urgency and greater tensions to fraught negotiations over who will bear the brunt of badly needed cuts. Seven states that have blown past two key deadlines are still locked in a stalemate over how the river’s essential resources will be managed through a hotter and drier future.But the dire snowpack numbers have pushed some municipalities to initiate early water restrictions. Local officials in Salt Lake City, Utah, have called on residents and businesses to begin conserving, with a goal to cut up to 10 m gallons, while city facilities will curb 10% of their use. Across Colorado, there are local orders that limit lawn watering, and in Wyoming residents were warned that full restrictions on outdoor irrigation could come as early as May. Farmers and ranchers across the west are also having to make hard decisions and big adjustments with smaller allocations of water and a recognition that supplies will be strained.A troubling outlook for fire seasonThe fast-melting snow is expected to have profound impacts on drinking water supply, agriculture production, and outdoor recreation. It could also set the stage for bigger blazes.“Unless there’s a major change in the weather patterns and we somehow pull out some sort of miracle springtime precipitation, we’re looking at an extended fire season,” said Dr Joel Lisonbee, senior associate scientist at the Cooperative Institute for Research at the University of Colorado Boulder, noting that there was not a one-to-one relationship between snowpack and fire, but they are connected.“In any sort of fire situation, you need some spark or ignition,” he said. Landscapes that would typically spend longer underneath a protective blanket of snow will become more primed to burn. Fire season may “begin weeks to months earlier than what we would usually expect”, he said. “These high temperatures and low snowpack will lead to a rapid drying of the vegetation that’s around, and that will lead to this early start.”Reising, Nemeth, Jim Shannon and Jacob Kollen found a zero measurement of snow during a snow survey at Phillips Station on 1 April 2026. This is the second lowest since 2015 during snow survey at the Phillips Station in the Sierra Nevada. Photograph: Paul Kitagaki Jr/ZUMA Press Wire/ShutterstockDozens of large destructive fires have already erupted in recent weeks across the Intermountain West and the High Plains, spurred by extreme heat and low moisture. More than 1.5m acres have already burned this year across the US, more than double the 10-year average.While Schumacher said he expects this year to be a standout one, the climate crisis is fueling warming trends that climate scientists have long warned will leave the west hotter and drier. Seasons with snow in the US west are shrinking while high fire risks stretch across more months.“Climate change is going to result in a lot of these extreme events worsening,” said Dr Abby Frazier, a climatologist and assistant professor at Clark University, who added that compound events, where hazards overlap or occur in quick succession, are on the rise. The heat and the drought this year, served as a one-two punch, and will work together to produce greater dangers from fire.She emphasized the need to take transformative action, and prioritize adaptation and mitigation. “It is heartbreaking to see it all playing out as we have predicted for so long,” she said. “The changes we have teed up for ourselves are going to be catastrophic.”

评论（默认中文）

bayarearefugee 2026/04/03 00:44
难道我们不应该“震惊”吗？ 过去11年是有记录以来最热的11年。

气候灾难即将来临。 每个人都知道这一点，即使是那些大声否认的人（那些有权力的人，而不是他们喂养宣传的羊）。 他们只是决定从中获利，而不是试图放慢速度。

“钻，宝贝，钻”、“清洁美丽的煤炭”的疯狂与想要入侵格陵兰岛和加拿大都有直接关系。

Shouldn't we be past being "stunned"? The past 11 years are the hottest 11 years on record.

Climate catastrophe is coming, soon. Everybody knows this, even the vocal deniers (the ones with power, not the sheep they feed propaganda to). They've simply decided to profit off it instead of trying to slow it down.

The "drill, baby, drill", "clean beautiful coal" lunacy and wanting to invade Greenland and Canada are all directly related.

在 HN 中查看
czinck 2026/04/03 01:54
是的，冬天变得越来越糟，但今年真的很糟糕，比任何预测都要糟糕得多。二月份我在帕克城，雪很少，看起来就像夏天，那是在本文讨论的温暖干燥天气之前。我确实是在 55 度的天气下徒步到达 2002/2034 年奥运会设施的，而且地面上没有积雪，而奥运会仍在米兰举行。我在帕克城，因为那里的雪比科罗拉多州多……
Yes, winters are getting worse and worse, but this year was really bad, way worse than any predictions. I was in Park City in February and there was so little snow it looked like summer, and that was before the streak of warm and dry weather this article is talking about. I literally hiked up to the 2002/2034 Olympic facilities in 55 degree weather with no snow on the ground, while the Olympics were still going on in Milan. And I was in Park City because they had more snow than Colorado...
在 HN 中查看
blipvert 2026/04/03 02:11
不记得确切的引述了，但《预兆 II》中有一些关于未来为争夺食物而进行的战争的内容（索恩公司参与了农业/化肥之类的事情）。

上次我看到它时，我想知道乌克兰冲突是否可能与控制“欧洲粮仓”有关。

Can’t remember the exact quote, but there is something in The Omen II about future wars being fought over food (Thorn corporation being involved in agriculture/fertiliser or something).

Last time that I saw it I wondered if the Ukraine conflict might be about control of the “Breadbasket of Europe” as much as anything.

在 HN 中查看
everdrive 2026/04/03 02:22
当然，托马斯·马尔萨斯在所有事情上都错了，我们只是继续需要不断增长的人口。
But of course Thomas Malthus was wrong about everything and we just keep need to growing populations.
在 HN 中查看
wak90 2026/04/03 07:24
事实上，你讽刺地试图说马尔萨斯在某些事情上是正确的，这很能说明这种情况。
It's pretty indicative of the situation that you're unironically trying to say Malthus was right about some things, actually.
在 HN 中查看
nradov 2026/04/03 02:38
对欧洲粮食供应的控制可能是普京决定入侵乌克兰的一个次要因素，但对于建立一个具有可防御战略纵深的更大的俄罗斯帝国来说，这是次要因素。乌克兰的大量小麦出口到埃及，由于战争，埃及的粮食成本大幅上涨。
Control over European food supplies might have been a minor factor in Putin's decision to invade Ukraine but that was secondary to establishing a greater Russian empire with defensible strategic depth. A lot of Ukraine's wheat exports went to Egypt and they have suffered significant food cost inflation due to the war.

realo 2026/04/03 01:14
北极熊正在北方被淹死。

我想说气候灾难已经来临……至少对他们来说是这样。

The polar bears are drowning up north.

I would say climate catastrophe is already here... at least for them.

hervature 2026/04/03 01:24
自 20 世纪 60 年代以来，北极熊的数量一直在稳步增长 [1]。基本上是原来的两倍。您使用的可伪造信息越多，您对这一事业的帮助就越少。

[1]- https://thegwpf.org/content/uploads/2024/02/Crockford-State-...

The polar bear population has steadily been increasing since the 1960s [1]. Basically double what it was. The more falsifiable information you use the less you are helping the cause.

[1] - https://thegwpf.org/content/uploads/2024/02/Crockford-State-...

在 HN 中查看
tinyplanets 2026/04/03 01:30
这份报告由全球变暖政策基金会发布，根据维基百科，该基金会“是一个在英国注册为慈善组织的否认气候变化的游说团体。其既定目标是挑战各国政府为减轻人为全球变暖而设想的所谓“极具破坏性和有害的政策”。全球变暖政策基金会及其一些知名成员个人实践和推动否认气候变化。”

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Global_Warming_Policy_Foun...

所以听起来他们（我假设你）肯定有一个你想要推动的议程。

This report was published by the Global Warming Policy Foundation, which according to Wikipedia "is a climate change denial lobby group registered as a charitable organisation in the United Kingdom. Its stated aims are to challenge what it calls "extremely damaging and harmful policies" envisaged by governments to mitigate anthropogenic global warming. The GWPF, and some of its prominent members individually, practise and promote climate change denial."

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Global_Warming_Policy_Foun...

So it sounds like they (and I assume you) definitely have an agenda you're trying to promote.

在 HN 中查看
billfor 2026/04/03 02:48
发布正确的事实，而不是争论来源。这是来自“正确”来源的最新报告。

https://www.iucn-pbsg.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/PBSG-St...

阅读这篇文章并解释为什么人口正在减少——他唯一指出的一点是事实并非如此。

Post the correct facts rather than arguing about the source. Here’s the most recent report from a “correct” source.

https://www.iucn-pbsg.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/PBSG-St...

Read that and explain why the population is decreasing — the only point he made was that it was not.

在 HN 中查看
hervature 2026/04/03 04:31
谢谢。您阅读了我的帖子的实质内容并真诚地与之互动。你是一个真正的HNer，如果你曾经在LV，我很乐意给你买瓶啤酒。
Thank you. You read my post for its substance and interacted with it in good faith. You are a true HNer and if you are ever in LV, I will gladly buy you a beer.
在 HN 中查看
hervature 2026/04/03 04:21
说实话，我只是查了一下报告，并没有注意到它来自那里。我唯一的议程是，这是唯一一份清楚地显示多年来不同研究的平均值和 CI 的报告。 WWF 链接到实际报告 [1]，可在 [2] 中找到。他们尽最大努力不表明人口数量要么稳定，要么在增加。如果你看一下下降情况，例如在戴维斯海峡，就会发现下降了 1%，95% 区间内下降了 0%。

不管怎样，我确实承认从该网站链接看起来不太好，但我所做的只是链接该报告，我不提倡他们网站上的任何其他内容。我更重要的观点是，气候变化界不需要北极熊来推动他们的观点。这是一个糟糕的例子，我们应该使用许多其他可验证的来源（冰盖损失、海平面上升、干旱等）之一来代替。

[1]- https://www.arcticwwf.org/wildlife/polar-bear/polar-bear-pop... [2]- https://www.iucn-pbsg.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/PBSG-St...

To be honest, I just looked up the report and did not not notice it came from there. My only agenda was that it was the only report that clearly showed the average and CI of the different studies throughout the years. WWF links to the actual report [1] which is found at [2]. They try their very hardest to not show that the population is either stable or increasing. If you look at decreases, for example in Davis Strait, it is a loss of 1% with 0% in the 95% interval.

Anyway, I do admit that linking from that website is not a good look but all I did was link the report and I am not advocating for anything else on their website. My larger point, the climate change community does not need the polar bears to drive their point. It is a bad example and we should use one of the many other verifiable sources (ice sheet loss, sea level rise, droughts, etc.) instead.

[1] - https://www.arcticwwf.org/wildlife/polar-bear/polar-bear-pop... [2] - https://www.iucn-pbsg.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/PBSG-St...

在 HN 中查看
ceejayoz 2026/04/03 01:43
说到可伪造的信息：

https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/factcheck/2023/01/27/fac...

> 专家表示，北极熊数量的增加反映了追踪北极熊的能力增强，而不是北极熊数量的实际增加。科学家称，该图基于对全球人口的各种估计，包括不科学的估计、外推法和数据集不足。

Speaking of falsifiable info:

https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/factcheck/2023/01/27/fac...

> Experts say the rising tally of polar bears reflects an increased ability to track bears – not an actual increase in the population. The graph is based on various estimates of the global population that include unscientific estimates, extrapolation and insufficient data sets, according to scientists.

在 HN 中查看
hervature 2026/04/03 04:29
你读过那篇文章吗？它说 1960 年至 1980 年的测量结果不可靠，因此该说法是错误的，但 1980 年以来的趋势仍然有效。你自己看看数据： https://www.iucn-pbsg.org/population-status/

你会发现全球人口一直稳定，他们自己说人口最多的地区（巴伦支海）很可能在过去 50 年里有所增加。

北极熊的数量是我最讨厌的，因为它是一个坏例子，如果你想继续捍卫它，那就继续吧，但你并没有帮助气候变化倡导者。

Did you even read that article? It says the measurements from 1960-1980 are unreliable so the claim is false but the trend still works from 1980. Go look at the data yourself: https://www.iucn-pbsg.org/population-status/

You will find that the population has been stable globally and they themselves say the most populated region (Barents sea) is has very likely increased in the last 50 years.

The polar bear population is a pet peeve of mine because it is a bad example, if you want to keep defending it, go ahead, but you are not helping climate change advocates.

在 HN 中查看
ceejayoz 2026/04/03 04:36
> 你会发现全球人口一直稳定……

这与您原来的主张非常不同。

你说它正在稳步增长，已经翻倍了。

是的，我读了整篇文章。

“北极熊国际组织的首席科学家史蒂文·阿姆斯特鲁普（Steven Amstrup）在一封电子邮件中表示：“‘北极熊的数量并没有增加。相反，我们不断增长的知识表明，这些地区的北极熊数量可能比我们之前想象的要多。’”

“数据最好的地区显示没有增加，这与帖子的说法相反。根据 2021 年的报告，其中三个亚种群在过去两代中有所减少。在过去两代中，没有一个亚种群有所增加。”

> You will find that the population has been stable globally…

That is a very different claim than your original.

You said it is steadily increasing and has doubled.

And, yes, I read the whole article.

"'Populations have not grown,' Steven Amstrup, chief scientist for Polar Bears International, said in an email. 'Rather our growing knowledge has shown there may be more bears in these areas than we previously thought.'"

"The areas with the best data show no increase, contrary to the post's claim. According to the 2021 report, three of the subpopulations have decreased over the past two generations. None of the subpopulations have increased over the past two generations."

在 HN 中查看
hervature 2026/04/03 04:50
需要明确的是，我没有改变我的主张。我只是指出，即使是北极熊人也说北极熊数量并未减少，并且出于某种原因拒绝说出他们自己的数据表明的全球人口正在增加。根据他们来自熊最多地区的数据：

亚群估计和不确定性 - 2644 (95% CI = 1899–3592)

长期变化 - 很可能增加（1973-2015）

这些说法并非我捏造。我正在阅读您所引用的人的文字和数据。

To be clear, I have not changed my claim. I am merely point out that even the polar bear people say that it is not in decline and, for some reason, refuse to say what their own data says, which is global population is on the rise. From their data from the region with most bears:

Subpopulation estimate and uncertainty - 2644 (95% CI = 1899–3592)

Long term change - Very likely increased (1973-2015)

I am not making up these claims. I am reading the very words and data from the people you are quoting.

在 HN 中查看
ceejayoz 2026/04/03 04:57
我正在阅读并引用您的话。

> 自 20 世纪 60 年代以来，北极熊的数量一直在稳步增加。基本上是原来的两倍。

然后：

> 你会发现全球人口一直稳定。

你能解决这两种说法之间明显的冲突吗？

I am reading and quoting your very words.

> The polar bear population has steadily been increasing since the 1960s. Basically double what it was.

Then:

> You will find that the population has been stable globally.

Can you resolve the apparent conflict between these two statements?

在 HN 中查看
hervature 2026/04/03 06:14
阅读第二部分之前的文字：

”“”

自己去看看数据：[链接]

你会发现全球人口一直稳定[...]

”“”

我总结一下自己的分析。如果你查看数据，你会发现全球人口正在增长。

Read the words before the second part:

"""

Go look at the data yourself: [link]

You will find that the population has been stable globally [...]

"""

I am summarizing their own analysis. If you go look in the data, you will see that the global population is on the rise.

在 HN 中查看
ceejayoz 2026/04/03 07:03
这是一个闪避。

我查看了您链接的数据。

列出的约20个地区人口中，1个表示长期增加，2个表示长期减少，其余均表示数据不足。

看起来和你的形象不太相符。

你从哪里得到“双”位？

That’s a dodge.

I looked at the data you linked.

Of the ~20 regional populations listed, one says long term increase, two say long term decrease, and the rest all say insufficient data.

It doesn’t seem to match up with your portrayal very well.

Where did you get the “doubled” bit from?

在 HN 中查看
hervature 2026/04/03 08:55
那不是躲闪。查看“一个长期增加”和“两个长期减少”并比较估计的人口。你有 2644 vs 618+900=1518。因此，如果其余种群的数据“不足”，而您只有上述数据可供参考，那么唯一合乎逻辑的结论是全球北极熊种群数量可能有所增加。

现在，对于翻倍，如果你看看我链接的原始研究，它有一个几十年来点估计的图表。从60年代到现在大约翻了一番。如果你因为“专家认为这是糟糕的数据”而抛弃 60 年代，那么即使增长仍然是 50%。这些是基于不同时期的多项研究的估计，而世界自然基金会的报告使用的是单一报告。

我已经充分捍卫了我的主张，并提供了除“专家说……”的新闻文章之外的其他内容的实际来源。如果您想解决任何索赔或提供真实数据，请随意。

That is not a dodge. Look at the "one long term increase" and "two long term decrease" and compare the estimated populations. You have 2644 vs 618+900=1518. So, if the rest of the population is "insufficient data" and you only have the above to go off of, the only logical conclusion is that global polar bear population has likely increased.

Now, for the doubling, if you look at the original study I linked, it has a graph of the point estimates through the decades. From the 60s to now is about a doubling. If you throw out the 60s because "it is bad data according to experts" then even the increase is still 50%. These are estimates based on multiple studies in the different time periods whereas the WWF report uses a single report.

I have sufficiently defended my claim and provided actual sources for things other than a news article that says "expert says...". If you want to address any claims or put forth real data, feel free.

在 HN 中查看
ceejayoz 2026/04/03 09:41
> 因此，如果其余种群的数据“不足”，而您只有上述数据可供参考，那么唯一合乎逻辑的结论是，全球北极熊种群数量可能有所增加。

一点也不。如果我在一条裤子中发现了 20 美元，那么合乎逻辑的结论并不是我的所有裤子都含有 20 美元。

> 如果你因为“根据专家的说法这是糟糕的数据”而抛弃 60 年代，那么即使增长仍然是 50%。

引用的专家还指出，80 年代的数字也存在同样的问题。

> 如果您想解决任何索赔或提供真实数据，请随意。

除非有时间机器，否则 20 世纪 60 年代的“真实数据”似乎……很难获得。

我们留下的人都知道自己在说什么，而且似乎广泛同意这一点。

> So, if the rest of the population is "insufficient data" and you only have the above to go off of, the only logical conclusion is that global polar bear population has likely increased.

Not at all. If I find a $20 in one single pair of pants, the logical conclusion is not that all of my pants have $20 in them.

> If you throw out the 60s because "it is bad data according to experts" then even the increase is still 50%.

The experts cited also indicate the 80s numbers have the same issue.

> If you want to address any claims or put forth real data, feel free.

Barring time machines, "real data" from the 1960s seems… tough to obtain.

Leaving us with people who know what they're talking about, who seem to widely agree on the point.

两者都可以是真的。
Both can be true.

patchorang 2026/04/03 01:39
这里有很多评论谈论比较二月/三月的图像是没有用的。这是有关正在发生的事情的数据。这个雪旅地点位于本文中犹他州的图片中。

https://www.cbrfc.noaa.gov/dbdata/station/swegraph/swegraph_...

There are quite a few comments here talking about how comparing images from feb/march isn’t useful. Here’s data on what’s going on. This snowtel location is within the Utah picture in the article.

https://www.cbrfc.noaa.gov/dbdata/station/swegraph/swegraph_...

UncleOxidant 2026/04/03 02:32
在哪里可以找到哥伦比亚河流域的类似数据？我怀疑，我们将在夏季中后期看到对水力发电产生巨大影响。对 PNW 和 CA 的影响。
Where can I find similar data for the Columbia River basin? That's where we're going to see large impacts on hydropower production come mid-to-late summer, I suspect. Impacts for the PNW and CA.
在 HN 中查看
patchorang 2026/04/03 02:53
在过滤器中，您可以更改 SNOTEL 站点。您可能需要调出一张 snotel 站点地图并在您感兴趣的地区找到一个。
In the filter you can change the SNOTEL site. You probably need to pull up a snotel site map and find one in the region you’re interested in.
在 HN 中查看
slwvx 2026/04/03 00:35
我拥有大盆地的土地和水资源股份，并且可以确认这是真实的。

从更积极的角度来看，该地区的一个随机人士出人意料地证实，他们认为全球变暖确实是真实的。

I own land and water shares in the Great Basin and can confirm that this is real.

On a more positive note, one random person in the area unexpectedly confirmed that they thought global warming was indeed real.

在 HN 中查看
saltcured 2026/04/03 02:27
我们的整个防洪和供水系统是围绕预期的雪储存水而设计的。

忽略可怕的干旱情景，如果我们开始暖冬并且冬季降水更多地以降雨形式出现，这种情况将会如何变化，这也令人不安。

我认为最糟糕的情况是，如果我们最终像一些热带国家一样，他们可能会在很短的周期内遭受灾难性的洪水和干旱。水一下子就来了，你无法控制或遏制它。但也存在一些差距，限制了储存足够水和管理消耗率的能力。

Our whole flood control and water supply system is designed around the expected storage of water as snow.

Ignoring horrifying drought scenarios, it is also troubling to think about how this will change if we start having warm winters and more of the winter precipitation as rain.

I think the worst case would be if we end up like some tropical countries, where they can have disastrous flooding and then drought in very short cycles. The water comes all at once and you cannot hope to control or contain it. But there are also gaps that strain the ability to store enough water and manage consumption rates.

在 HN 中查看
scarecrowbob 2026/04/03 07:30
我在科罗拉多州的西坡。 去年夏天，我们有 2 个月没有降水，然后一天就降水 6 英寸。哇……非常有趣。

更好的是，在像新墨西哥州鲁伊多索（我住过的地方）这样的地方，野火造成了相当大的森林砍伐，结果是每次下雨都会洪水泛滥。

在过去的 4 周里，我每天花费大约 3-5 小时试图将燃烧的东西尽可能远离我的棚屋，但我敢打赌，当它燃烧时，它会变得很大。

对此并不期待。

I'm on Colorado's Western Slope. Last summer we got about no precipitation for 2 months and then 6" in one day. Wooo... very fun.

Even better, in some place like Ruidoso, NM (where I've lived) there have been pretty massive deforestations from wildfires with the result being that it floods about any time it rains.

I've spent about 3-5hr/day for the last 4 weeks trying to get rid of stuff that burns as far out from my shacks as I can, but I would bet that when it burns, it's going to go big.

Not looking forward to that.

在 HN 中查看
lazystar 2026/04/03 00:47
我不认为将 2026 年 2 月的积雪覆盖率照片与 2026 年 3 月同一地区的积雪覆盖率照片进行比较有什么意义。3 月是春季，当然积雪覆盖率会更差。 如果积雪面积增加的话，那就更令人震惊了。 他们应该显示 2026 年 3 月与 2025 年 3 月/2024/2023 年等。
I don't see the point in comparing photos of snow coverage in feb 2026 to the same area in march 2026. March is a spring month, of course snow coverage will be worse. Itd be more shocking if the snow coverage increased. they should show march 2026 vs. march 2025/2024/2023 etc.
在 HN 中查看
margalabargala 2026/04/03 00:59
三月可能是您居住的地方的“春季月”，也是基于春分的“春季月”，但在美国西部，据统计，积雪高峰出现在三月底。

在判断此类事情是否异常时，请使用数字，而不是共鸣。因为三月是“春天的月份”而忽略这一点，就像问住在迈阿密的人是否认为二月地面没有雪是不寻常的。

March might be a "spring month" where you live, and also a "spring month" based on the equinox, but in the American West, peak snow pack statistically occurs at the end of March.

Use numbers, not vibes, when deciding if something like this is unusual. Dismissing this because march is a "spring month" is like asking someone who lives in Miami if they consider it unusual to have no snow on the ground in February.

在 HN 中查看
ctoa 2026/04/03 00:54
我所在的山脉地区，三月的降雪量通常与十二月/一月/二月的降雪量非常接近，而且积雪仍在增加，而不是减少。三月下旬通常是深度高峰。 3 月平均降雪量为 62 英寸，今年降雪量为 1 英寸，是有记录以来最干燥的三月，而且天气异常温暖。
Where I'm at in the Sierra, March is typically very close to as snowy as Dec/Jan/Feb and the snowpack is still increasing, not decreasing. Late March is typically the peak depth. March avg snowfall is 62", this year we got 1", the driest March on record, on top of it being incredibly warm.
在 HN 中查看
lapetitejort 2026/04/03 01:11
作为一个天真的游客，我并不知道这一点。 2011 年 3 月，我开车前往红杉国家公园，希望能够露营。道路已被犁过，道路两侧都有八英尺高的积雪。我开车到游客中心询问在哪里露营。公园管理员说我可以在任何我想要的地方露营。也许他认为我知道自己在做什么。但我没有。在停车场逛了一会儿，无处可去，我就开车出去了。
As a naive tourist, I did not know this. I drove up to Sequoia National Park in March 2011 hoping to camp. The roads were plowed, with eight feet tall snow on either side of the road. I drove up to a visitor center and asked where to camp. The park ranger said I could camp anywhere I wanted. Maybe he assumed I knew what I was doing. But I did not. After walking around the parking lot for a bit, with nowhere else to go, I drove out.
在 HN 中查看
chabes 2026/04/03 02:05
2011年也是一个大雪年。 2011年8月我在高原地区。缪尔山口是一片巨大的雪原。
2011 was a big snow year too. I was in the high country in August of 2011. Muir Pass was a huge snow field.
在 HN 中查看
Reubachi 2026/04/03 00:59
相反，东部地区是最近有记录以来最潮湿/最多雪的一月至三月。今天，四月二日，新罕布什尔州沿海地区下雪了。

当然，这并不是与西部缺雪相反，而且很可能实际上是“同一问题”的另一面。但很有趣

Conversly in east, wettest/snowiest jan-march on recent record. Today, april second, we got snow in coastal new hampshire.

Which of course isn't an antithesis to the lack of snow in the west, and likely is literally the flip side of the "same problem". but interesting

在 HN 中查看
rconti 2026/04/03 02:23
4 月 1 日，Sierra 地区也下了 11 英寸的雪！
11" of snow in the Sierra on April 1, as well!
在 HN 中查看
ctoa 2026/04/03 02:29
四月的降雪也很典型。根据目前的预测，四月份的降雪量也将远远低于正常水平。
April snowfall is also typical. With current forecasts, we will be far short of normal April snowfall as well.
在 HN 中查看
darth_avocado 2026/04/03 01:57
> 三月是春天，雪覆盖率当然会差一些。

西部（加利福尼亚州）的积雪高峰预计将在四月初出现。 12 月是降雨量密集的月份，积雪量趋于高于平均水平，但随后干燥的 2 月和 3 月的热浪不仅确保了积雪不会增长，而且几乎摧毁了早季降雨带来的任何覆盖物。令人震惊的是，12 月的降雪看起来像是历史性的雪，但在 3 个月内就陷入了灾难性的短缺。

> March is a spring month, of course snow coverage will be worse.

Peak snow cover in the west (California) is expected to be in early April. December was an intense month of rainfall and the snowpack was trending towards above average, but then a dry Feb and a heatwave in March not only ensured the pack didn’t grow but pretty much nuked whatever cover early season rains brought. It is shocking because in December it was looking like historical snow and it went into catastrophic shortage in 3 months.

在 HN 中查看
georgeburdell 2026/04/03 00:51
加州积雪高峰历史上是 4 月 1 日。
California peak snowpack is historically April 1.
在 HN 中查看
[deleted] 2026/04/03 01:18 deleted

[已删除]

[deleted]

在 HN 中查看
mikestew 2026/04/03 00:54
“三月通常是暴风雪的大月份，”舒马赫说。 “我们通常期望的不是下雪，而是前所未有的、超乎寻常的温暖。” （https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2026/apr/01/snowmelt-ame...）

除此之外，测量的是剩余的水分融化量：

专家们担心的不仅仅是山顶上的积雪量，还有山顶上仍然冻结的水分含量。 “雪水当量”（SWE）是衡量融化后供应自然和人造系统的指标，该值非常低。

“March is often a big month for snowstorms,” Schumacher said. “Instead of getting snow we would normally expect we got this unprecedented, way-off-the-scale warmth.” (https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2026/apr/01/snowmelt-ame...)

Beside that, the measurements are of how much moisture is left to melt off:

It’s not just the amount of snow left on mountaintops that’s concerning experts, but the amount of moisture still frozen within them. “Snow water equivalent” (SWE), a measurement of what could melt off to supply natural and manmade systems, is exceptionally low.

在 HN 中查看
neura 2026/04/03 01:00
是的，如果除了 2026 年 2 月与 3 月重复但略有不同的观点之外，实际上还有任何其他比较，那就太好了。
Yes, it would be great if there were literally any other comparison other than repeated but slightly different views of Feb vs Mar in 2026 only.
在 HN 中查看
rconti 2026/04/03 02:25
犹他州和科罗拉多州度过了一个糟糕的冬天，画上句号。

加州12月的表现相当不错。然后2月底3月初，高空的一场降雨融化了很多积雪，随后3月中下旬的一场并不罕见的热浪融化了很多剩下的积雪。

Utah and Colorado had an awful winter, full stop.

California did quite well in December. Then late February and early March came along, and a rain event at high altitude melted a lot of the snowpack, followed by a not-uncommon heatwave in mid-late March melted a lot of what was left.

在 HN 中查看
micromacrofoot 2026/04/03 00:49
事实上，在这些海拔地区，三月份的降雪量更多，直到春季晚些时候才会融化……所以虽然我同意直接进行月份比较会很高兴看到……但这仍然很重要。

> 山脉的积雪融化得如此之快，如果按照目前的速度继续下去，到四月初就会所剩无几。斯温表示，虽然不太可能保持这种惊人的速度，但该州仍有很大可能出现有记录以来最早的融化。

> “感觉我们今年跳过了春天，直接陷入了夏季热浪，”DWR 主任卡拉·内梅斯 (Karla Nemeth) 在周三的新闻发布会上说道。 “本来应该逐渐融化的雪在几周前突然发生了。”她补充说，今年的调查是他们进行的最快的调查之一。

所以这里的警报是融化速度，它应该持续更长的时间。这是一个问题，因为这是下游水源的天然“储存”……如果提前全部释放，它会蒸发得更快，并且在整个季节都不会得到更多的融化补充。

They actually get more snow in March at these elevations, it doesn't melt until later in the spring... so while I agree a direct month comparison would be nice to see... this is still significant.

> The snow is melting so fast in the Sierra that, if it continues at its current rate, little would be left by early April. It’s unlikely to keep up this astounding pace, but there’s still high potential for the earliest melt-off on record in the state, according to Swain.

> “It feels like we skipped spring this year and dropped straight into a summer heatwave,” said Karla Nemeth, the DWR director, during Wednesday’s briefing. “What should be gradual snowmelt happened suddenly weeks ago.” This year’s was one of the quickest surveys they’d had, she added.

So the alarm here is the rate of melt, it should be sustained over a longer period. This is a problem because this is a natural "store" of water for downstream sources... if it's all released earlier it evaporates quicker and isn't replenished with more melt throughout the season.

在 HN 中查看
royal__ 2026/04/03 08:03
在犹他州，三月的降雪量甚至不及四月的降雪量，这是不寻常的。山上的积雪应该会一直持续到八月。今年的野火情况可能会非常糟糕。
In Utah it's atypical to not have just as much if not more snow in March than in April. The snow pack in the mountains should last all the way until August. This year will likely be very bad for wildfires.
在 HN 中查看
vuggamie 2026/04/03 02:21
春天从三月的最后一周开始。 3 月份，Lazystar 身穿花卉印花连衣裙亮相，看起来绝对是一款工具。享受你的火车套装吧！
Spring starts the last week of March. lazystar out there in floral print dresses on March first looking like an absolute tool. Enjoy your train set!
在 HN 中查看
royal__ 2026/04/03 08:01
在犹他州，通常有同样的粘液
In Utah its typical to have just as muc
在 HN 中查看
singleshot_ 2026/04/03 01:40
三月是海平面上的春季月份。
March is a spring month at sea level.
在 HN 中查看
vuggamie 2026/04/03 02:24
令人难以置信的观察力。根据海平面的不同，季节的开始和结束日期也不同。我的印象是，三月的前三分之二在北半球的每个海拔高度都是冬天。 singleshot_ 为这次对话增添了很多内容。你无法区分 AI slopposters 和普通 hn 用户，因为他们出门的时间都是相同的。
Incredible observation. Seasons begin and end on different days depending on sea level. I was under the impression that the first two thirds of March was winter at every elevation in the northern hemisphere. singleshot_ has added much to this conversation. You cannot tell AI slopposters from regular hn users because they all get out of the house the same amount of time.
在 HN 中查看

ourmandave 2026/04/03 01:14

I was naively hoping they were stunned because it wasn't as much as they expected.

bix6 2026/04/03 02:10

Colorado river collapsing this year?
在 HN 中查看
bpt3 2026/04/03 01:38
我不得不问，因为这对我来说毫无意义：在文章中，有一张 3 名工人进行雪调查并发现“零测量雪”的照片。据报道，这是“自 2015 年以来的第二低”。

物品数量的任何测量如何小于零？据我所知，你不可能有负面的雪。

I have to ask because this makes no sense to me: In the article, there is a picture of 3 workers taking a snow survey and finding "zero measurement of snow". This is reported as "the second lowest since 2015".

How is any measurement of a quantity of an item less than zero? You can't have negative snow to my knowledge.

在 HN 中查看
munificent 2026/04/03 02:10
这篇文章的文笔确实不太好。

我相信他们所说的是 2015 年的测量结果也是零。因此，今年的测量结果不是“第二低”，而是“第二低”。这是我能解释它的唯一方式。

The writing in this article indeed isn't great.

I believe what they're saying is that the 2015 measurement was also zero. So this year's measurement isn't the "second-lowest", it's the "second equally lowest". That's the only way I could interpret it.

在 HN 中查看
shagmin 2026/04/03 02:28
我只是假设他们的意思是与 2015 年相比，本赛季的累计降雪量不再增加。
I just kind of assumed they meant no additional snow to add to the accumulated amount they have for the season compared to 2015.

FpUser 2026/04/03 01:41
伙计们，我们有大量的水，非常多。最好的水。你看看加拿大：美丽的水，大水……它就坐在那里！不公平，不公平。我们应该得到一些水。这将是巨大的。
We have tremendous water, folks, tremendous. The best water. You look at Canada: beautiful water, big water ... It’s just sitting there! Not fair, not fair. We should be getting some of that water. It’ll be huge.

mschaef 2026/04/03 02:30
从它的声音来看，你可能已经知道了这一点，但是 1950 年代有一个计划要做一些与你在这里暗示的非常相似的事情：

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_American_Water_and_Power...

From the sound of it, You may know this already, but there was a 1950's era plan to do something very similar to what you imply here:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_American_Water_and_Power...
